figures.caption: Table 1: Subject-wise performance (WAR, UAR) on the BioVid dataset for TTA-CaP and other TTA methods.
Table 4: Subject-wise performance (F1 score) on the BAH dataset for TTA-CaP and other TTA methods. Best F1 scores are highlighted in bold.
Table 3: Subject-wise performance (F1 score) on the StressID dataset for TTA-CaP and other TTA methods. Best F1 scores are highlighted in bold.

Fig. 5: (a) Average WAR for target subjects in BioVid for zero-shot CLIP, static personalized source cache only, dynamic target cache only, and TTA-CaP (static + dynamic). (b) Ablation of source-cache construction in BioVid. Average WAR across target subjects. Prototypes: the personalized source cache built from source-subject prototypes.

Fig. 3: Qualitative visualization. Left: t-SNE visualization of the embedding space before/after cache fusion for two subjects from StressID. Right: gate pass rates (fraction of frames passing each criterion and updating the caches).

Fig. 2: Overview of our TTA-CaP. (top) Offline source prototype extraction. For each source-domain subject, CLIP image embeddings are clustered, and representative prototypes are selected to define a labeled set of source prototypes. (bottom) Online TTA and tri-gate cache update. At test time, frozen CLIP encoders produce text and visual embeddings and an initial prediction through contrastive similarity. A fixed personalized source cache retrieves the top-m nearest source prototypes as a stable reference. A tri-gate controls updates to a dynamic target cache with positive and negative entries. Finally, predictions are obtained by embedding-level fusion of the CLIP visual embedding with retrieved cache items, enabling cost-effective personalization without training (backpropagation) at test-time.
